title: Dog Torn Nail: Bleeding and Limping First Aid & Triage Guide
meta_desc: Find out what to do if your dog has a torn nail, is bleeding, or limping. Learn about urgency levels and how to provide immediate first aid.
content_html:
What is a Torn Nail?

A torn or broken nail occurs when a dog's claw catches on a surface, causing it to split, crack, or partially detach. This injury often exposes the 'quick,' which is the sensitive core of the nail containing blood vessels and nerves. Because the quick is directly linked to the nervous system and is very vascular, these injuries are famously painful and can cause a surprising amount of blood loss and visible limping.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for a torn nail is Medium. While it is not usually a life-threatening emergency, it is a painful condition that carries a high risk of infection if left untreated. If the bleeding is profuse and does not stop after 15 minutes of direct pressure, or if your dog is acting excessively lethargic, you should seek immediate care. Otherwise, you should aim to see a veterinarian within 12 to 24 hours to have the damaged nail safely trimmed and to begin pain management.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If you are consulting a vet via a triage service, a clear photo of the injured paw is essential. A visual allows a professional to determine the severity of the split and see if the nail is still attached or dangling. It also helps them assess the 'quick' to see if it is exposed or damaged, which helps them decide whether your dog needs immediate sedation for a procedure or if simple bandaging and antibiotics will suffice.

faqs.question: How can I stop the bleeding at home?
faqs.answer: Apply firm, steady pressure with a clean cloth for at least 5-10 minutes. If bleeding persists, you can apply styptic powder, or a household substitute like cornstarch or flour, to the tip of the nail to help it clot.

faqs.question: Why is my dog limping from just a broken nail?
faqs.answer: The 'quick' of the nail is attached to the bone of the toe. When the nail is torn, it creates a constant throbbing sensation and nerve pain every time the foot touches the ground, leading to a limp.

faqs.question: Should I try to cut the hanging nail off myself?
faqs.answer: It is generally best to let a professional do this. Cutting a torn nail can be extremely painful and may cause your dog to bite out of reflex. Vets often use local numbing agents to remove the damaged piece safely.
